item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could swear there was another skip that has a very similar idea, (Ghosts trapped in retail hell) but was done a lot better. Really made you feel the existential dread of spending eternity doing the same boring job.

This isn't bad, it just could be so much more. Love the idea and some of the details are great.

+1 but honestly not very enthusiastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was re-written about five years ago after dipping down to deletion range, and today sits at a mere +18.
Looking at the vote history, it seems to have gotten a fair amount of attention. The low score is more a result of strictly divided opinion.

To me, this points to needing a few tweaks to nudge the balance of voting toward a positive trend.

One such small tweak would be cutting the vanishing observers/cameras. That was always a worn out cliché and time has not improved it. The gimmick also doesn't add anything. It would be just as mysterious and spooky if the change just happened; all right there on camera and leaving staff just standing in a once-again ruined store.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That place on the lower left of the picture looks like a sword with a jewel in its hilt. Because Pareidolia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. Really really great. Short, powerful and makes you think. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels to me like they're sort of… brainwashed? Like they're real people but trapped in a dimension that only sort of touches ours, and sometimes they break this behavioral pattern they're trapped in and their dread takes over. They try to escape, or they resign themselves to their horror, but they can't get out and their old patterns take over again.

But that's just the idea that I got from reading this. YMMV.

Edit: I would be stoked if the author expanded this a little, as reading over the comment thread on here I found that they are, indeed, aware of their continued existence in a soul-crushingly tedious alternate reality. Seriously, I feel this would do amazingly with a few more paragraphs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless ObserverSeptember decides to rewrite it, of course. Authors can always rewrite or delete regardless of the rating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As soon as it drops to deletion range again, by the looks of things.

Rewrites occur as a response to deletion votes. If an article is voted for deletion, someone who thinks they can salvage the article requests for a rewrite (after getting permission from the original author).

This article managed to save itself as-is, since it no longer qualified for deletion. No deletion, no rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So there's a rewrite happening?
…When?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like having being so harsh but this is just terrible. There is no hook, it uses a concept that seems to have been done hundreds of time and it doesn't do it well, plus the second addendum doesn't really add anything. It's not creepy or intriguing or anything. It just makes me go "… okay".

Also…

Samuel Clark Properties

*sigh*… no.

recording equipment (including personnel tracking devices) placed in the interior spontaneously fails, and personnel present during the shift have never been recovered.

Oh good, another SCP that randomly has the power to deactivate recording and tracking devices and make people disappear for no reason other than to have an excuse for why the Foundation doesn't know what happens. I thought we needed another one of those to add to the pile.

It's also very awkwardly worded. It took me a while to realize the author probably meant that people and equipment introduced before the inactive phase disappear or fail as at first it gave me the impression that the inactive phase WAS the shift, so I was wondering how the SCP Foundation knew what the building looked like during that phase if they have no way to observe it or have it described.

Bah, humbug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apparently this has been resuced from the negatives. Yay!

A bit of author commentary: at the time I was writing this, I was working a soulless minimum wage job at a discount retail store. I went with the old adage of "think of what scares you,"; at the time, nothing scared me more than the idea of working in retail for all eternity.

So, Word of God: All of these creatures are sentient, and aware of every moment they spend in here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though this may not be the most original SCP, it has a lot of potential. I feel there's a lot more meaning behind this than what is currently written. I hope a rewrite will really bring forth what this SCP can be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is extremely underrated, upvoting for that reason.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
There is a rewrite happening, hold the phone on this one.

There is also an account deleted downvote.

item.title: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
Shall we?

Anyone wanting to ask for the opportunity to rewrite may respond to this post. Do not 'vote' if you are not staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly not a bad scip. I like the idea hinted at here but, like many others, I just don't think there is enough to it. Also, how did the place stay secret? I can understand that the Foundation would figure out about this place quickly, but there had to have been at least a few people who had roamed in before the Foundation came. Either way, I see the reasoning for both sides on this one. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would really like to try to rewrite this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does nothing for me sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like others said, it's just too plain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

Just… I like it.

I do have to agree; this definately could use some expansion to it, but the core concept is good and easily workable. I would most defibately want to see an expanded-upon, if not rewritten version of this.

Feeling generous. +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I re-rated this, not sure why I rated it up before as this just doesn't have enough to it. There not enough story and it's just random ghosts doing things. There's mystery but there's either not enough of it or too much of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see enough here. Random ghosts needs more story. Story. Story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd have to agree with a lot of the comments here. It's not bad, but it's not good, either. Nothing very exciting or original about it, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is a tad bare, I'll admit, but only because I couldn't think of a backstory to fit in. I wrote a backstory for it in "The Leak" thread, but that was more of a non-canonical interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like your whole "eternity in retail" idea you have, and I think it's nice and original. So I upvoted based on that. But the article kind of just ends and I can't tell where it's supposed to go after that. So I'm stuck in a kind of limbo between creeped out, confused, and wanting to know more, and sometimes that's a good thing. But it doesn't seem right here, and it feels like it deserves more explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with the "meh" crowd on this one. I see a hint of a good SCP here, but it needs to be refined and fleshed out. There are the minor issues people have mentioned, but the major one is, as previously stated, the lack of a good hook.

While reading it, I was sort of wondering where you were going with it. I came to the conclusion that you were trying to make something that scares even the ghosts… like an invisible ghost eating monster or a ghost sickness. If that's what you are going for, you need to make things a little creepier. Maybe add 2-3 more ghosts and have them get truly horrified at something. Maybe even 'kill' one of them.

Your post seems to indicate that's NOT what you are going for, that you are actually going for "Eternity in Retail Hell" in which case I think you to go less creepy and more funny/sad/true. Have worker ghosts slack off until manager ghost comes by. Have cashier ghost get mad at customers and throw nonexistent money back at them. Maybe have a ghost try to commit suicide, only to fail because he's, well, a ghost.

Whatever you do, you need more than what you have. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I spent a few months in Detroit some years ago and that's one detail that got me interested. There's definitely something ghostly and creepy about that place. +1, because I liked it and it's well written. But it could be improved, like most people say.

Personally, I would remove the: ("squatting"). Generally I think common usage words in parenthesis are only useful when most people would not understand the terms otherwise. Also it just looks weird to me to have simultaneous parenthesis and quotations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. (And I like the setting of urban Detroit, because yes, there is something very specific to Detroit that you don't get from anywhere else other than maybe Gary.) ;)

I see you doing a lot of handwaving in the comments here as people pick out stuff that isn't essential to your premise, and I think that's because your description of events could cover a variety of supernatural tropes. I read it as overlapping-dimension-going-to-chaos, a lot of others are reading it as ghosts, and I'm not altogether sure what you're aiming for with the concept "eternity in retail" whether the people within are dead or alive or in limbo or what.

If there's a flash of light when it flips back, that's something relevant to the set-up that belongs in the data log. I'm not certain why the cameras disappear if the set is mostly incorporeal, other than to save you from having to show in the research log what happens. Though I will say the first bit of creepies I felt reading this was hearing that people in our world disappear when interacting with this building at the wrong time. Where do they go? Can they be retrieved? Even if the Foundation wouldn't try, would one of the locals who lost a friend or lover? And what stuck these people in retail hell for eternity to begin with? I'm not saying you need to answer all these things in the article (please don't), but if you at least knew for yourself what the answer was, you could tighten up this text in a way that would send our imaginations right to the same specific area without too much length or heavy-handedness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll agree with Scantron and say that this one is just…very easy. The execution doesn't add enough to move past the fairly obvious concept. It's not unintelligent, and it certainly isn't bad, but it's too bland to get an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, but also like how it is now. The appetite is whetted, but not satisfied. This can be good or bad, especially with SCPs. I'd consider a minor expansion of it, maybe descriptions of more of the "ghosts", or more info on why the store closed down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In all honesty, the concept of an eternal routine is absolutely horrible, but it's not clear enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of… Nothing happened.

And nothing happens1.

Also, "people caught in transition get purged from reality" and "equipment failed" seem a bit overused and are, frankly, cop outs (reasonable ones at that, but with an article that bare-bones, an interesting take on transition between "normal" and "anomalous" state would have helped a lot)

Footnotes
1. Zat is ze djouk, I get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All digital equipment fails when attempting to record it. As for observation from outside…*handwaves* let's just say there's a flash of light or summat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. Lowest rated SCP of mine to date that's lasted 24 hours(ish).

Surprised at how divisive this was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attempts to observe SCP-1775 during this transition have been met with failure: recording equipment (including personnel tracking devices) placed in the interior spontaneously fails, and personnel present during the shift have never been recovered.

It can't be observed from the outside? Like keep a door open and put the camera and lights outside?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it's well written, I just don't really see any appeal in this article. There's really nothing that sets this apart from a standard haunted building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … alright, now you have me comparing this to Giraffe Hell…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First thing's first: think of what scares you

My answer was "working in retail for all eternity." So yeah, it's a bit grimdark, but that's only 'cause there have been many occasions during my job where I have wanted to run into a wall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda heavy-handed, innit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The genesis and inspiration for this article came from the mind-numbing repetitiveness of the last year of my life as a retail employee. Needless to say, I am looking forward to quitting next month.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…typical of Detroit's urban decay…" This implies to me that there is something uniquely identifiable about the derelicts of Detroit. That an expert could identify which city a relic came from based on the way the paint peeled or some such, like a wine snob telling if an unlabeled vitnage was from Napa or Naples. While that's an amusing concept, it may be a slight tone slip. Naming Detroit three times in the first three sentences is also a bit repetitive.

In the 2nd paragraph of the description, repairs are made but no new material is added. So what happens if a beam is destroyed or removed? Is there just a gap in the otherwise repaired interior facade?

"…have been met with failure…" is the sort of unnecessary embellishment that I tend to add when I'm not paying attention. In this case "have failed" would work just as well, and tightening the text is always a good thing.

End of the day shift can not be observed, but does the equipment also fail if it's present for the start of day shift?

I may be wrong, but I think military time never has a colon between hours and minutes.

What happens to observation attempts from outside at 8:00 p.m.? Do cameras looking in the windows also fail like the ones placed inside?

Final decision: +1.
All errors above are minor (or possibly not errors), and you've done a good job of distilling that feeling of "I'll be doing the same thing forever" ennui that comes from working retail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I despise one word article reviews with the fury of a thousand expolding suns. However, in this case I can only really say "meh". It is well written and all, don't get me wrong. It's just bland, tasteless, and boring.

Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this pretty much tasteless (and I don't think the part where their behavior change is that creepy). I supose the hook is to see how these "ghosts" get frustrated or something and did something different… I'm still failing to see the creepness in the article. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't want this to be a lot longer, but I would like just a little more. I liked where it was heading (particularly the foetal ball), but then it just… ended.

Neutral vote for now, but leaning upvote with a teensy bit more polishing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok then.
This did feel a little cliche, but this felt short and sweet to me. I mean I actually felt like I was eating a cupcake while reading this.
I'll give it my +1.

Edit: maybe its because one guy kept his face in his hands for 30 minutes, while the other punched the wall for hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It waves automatically whenever someone enters the store. This is the only way that the entities "interact" with someone, but it doesn't really count as interaction since it's more a script/reflex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Description: Stands near the entrance to the store, holding its hands behind its back. When a person enters the store, it will briefly wave at them before returning to its previous stance. To date, it has never shown any signs of movement unless a person enters the store.

Wait, it waves at living people? Or does it wave at other manifestations that walk into the store?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's creepy, but it needs more of a story. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. There isn't really much of a hint at, well anything. While I'm fine with mysterious, if that's the angle you're going for it needs to be something more interesting than standard ghosts to hold my attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is way too plain vanilla for my liking. It seems to borrow elements from other skips without bringing anything particularly new or inventive to the table.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty standard Twilight Zone style ghost story. I'm afraid I don't find any interest here.

-1. Written fine, but the idea is not executed with sufficient originality for me to enjoy it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Assuming there actually is a wall there (one of the hallmarks of ghosts is that the "walking through walls" is generally considered to be a case of the ghost walking through a door that isn't there anymore), probably your best case is to indicate that it appears to have collided with the wall and possibly a note at the end indicating that the wall shows no effect from the repeated impacts (under the heading of "wall that wasn't there at the time", and assuming they're punching hard into your typical drywall / light wood / etc wall, and assuming someone was close enough to observe, you could note that the hands briefly passed into the wall as if a hole or dent had been punched into it) to reinforce that no, it's still intangible despite it seemingly interacting with the environment.

Of course, it's also possible that the wall there doesn't exist due to it having crumbled away, etc (the description in the article is slightly confusing to me with how it notes that paint seems to appear on the walls, support columns no longer crumbling, etc, but then notes that no matter is added. Is it just a veneer of illusion? Does new paint and mortar appear and then disappear at the end? Have the Foundation tried to take samples if it's not illusory? Have they tried senses outside of the human range?) in which case you simply note that the figure collides with the location where the wall used to be and is punching that empty space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not actually punching it; intangible and all that.

Suggestions on rewording?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… so the intangible ghost thing is punching the walls? Or is he just making punching motions in the direction of the wall? The current phrasing makes it sound like he's actually striking the physical wall.

I'm not really getting the hook either. It's the ghosts of department store workers who occasionally seem dissatisfied with their job. Or something. It just doesn't pull me in. I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think a lot more could have been done with this. You gave yourself an idea that you really could have run with but kept it too simple in my opinion. No vote for now because it's a good simple article, but still simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of minor things:

  • "████ department store" — If this is a proper name, it should be capitalized.
  • "makeshift sleeping bags" — This doesn't make sense to me. Should it be "makeshift bedding" instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it's ghost-shop part that's creepy, it's the transition in behaviour for me that made it creepy. I think it's the kind of happy atmosphere from the first log broken up by bits of sad/depressing atmosphere from the second log. It's like the latter is being covered up by the former. That sort of thing creeps me out but maybe that's just me.

Apologies if I've explained this terribly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted because while I loves me some ghosty type stuff, you didn't do enough with it. There's a start here, but where the reenacted scenes in SCP-1382 left my brain full of fuck, this just fizzles out.

Might change my vote if that changes, but for now it's a -1.

Edit: I'm not seeing the creepiness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last experiment log makes this incredibly creepy. I like it. +1
